title: Trinidadian curry chicken
subtitle: Bone-in chicken braised in Trinidadian curry with fresh herbs and tomato
excerpt: Chicken pieces braised in a Trinidadian-style curry: less coconut milk than the Jamaican version, more tomato and fresh coriander, with a distinctive green seasoning base of spring onion, garlic, and thyme.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian curry chicken differs from Jamaican in its base ingredients: Trinidadian cooking uses a green seasoning marinade of fresh herbs (shadow beni, thyme, spring onion, garlic) and incorporates fresh tomato rather than relying heavily on coconut milk. The curry style is Indo-Trinidadian in character, using a Trinidad-specific curry powder blend (Chief brand is standard) that is more cumin-forward than Jamaican curry powder. This recipe uses standard curry powder with additional cumin. Shadow beni (culantro) is replaced by fresh coriander.

content.text: Trinidadian curry chicken is a different dish from the Jamaican version. The base is different. Coconut milk plays a smaller part. Fresh tomato and coriander give it a greener, brighter character. In Trinidad, most cooks start with a green seasoning marinade: fresh herbs, garlic, and spring onion. The chicken sits in that marinade for at least 30 minutes before the curry powder goes in.

The technique of 'burning the curry' is specific to Trinidadian cooking: the curry powder is added to the dry, hot pot and toasted briefly before any liquid is added. This drives off the raw starchy notes and deepens the flavour. Watch it, 30 seconds is enough; much longer and it scorches.

Blend the {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{coriander}} of coriander, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, and {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet with 2 tablespoons of water to a rough green paste. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Rub all over the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs and {{chicken-drumsticks}} drumsticks. Leave for at least 30 minutes, or overnight in the fridge.
Burn the curry and brown the chicken

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Add the {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and {{cumin-ground}} of cumin to the hot, dry pot (push the oil to one side). Toast for 30 seconds, stirring, until fragrant. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook in the spiced oil for 4 minutes. Add the chicken pieces and brown on all sides, about 5 minutes total.

Add the {{tomato}} tomatoes and 200 ml of water. Stir, scraping up any browned bits from the base. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 25 minutes. If using {{potato}} potatoes, add them now and cook for a further 20 minutes until tender and the sauce has reduced. Taste for salt before serving.

items.fix: Add the curry powder to the oil after it is hot, not to a dry pot. Keep it moving and watch carefully, 30 seconds is enough.
items.cause: Heat too high; pot too dry
items.symptom: Curry powder burning rather than toasting

items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 15 minutes to reduce. The tomatoes and chicken juices will thicken the sauce without any additional starch.
items.cause: Too much water; lid on throughout

items.fix: The green seasoning needs at least 30 minutes to penetrate. Overnight in the fridge is noticeably better. The flavour difference between 30 minutes and 8 hours is significant.
Add potatoes in the final 20 minutes only. Cut into large chunks (5 cm) rather than small dice.
items.cause: Added too early; cut too small

content.text: Trinidadian curry chicken is Sunday food, eaten with dhalpuri roti or white rice and a side of fried plantain. It is the dish that most clearly reflects the Indo-Trinidadian contribution to the island's cooking. The curry technique, the spice blend, and the green seasoning all come from the Indian indenture community. Over generations the dish also took on Creole elements: the scotch bonnet, the thyme, the technique of burning the curry. The result is something entirely of Trinidad.
